children.value: In this assignment you will write your first SQL queries against a manga library database. The goal is to get comfortable with the core building blocks of SQL: selecting data, filtering it, and sorting it.
You will work with a PostgreSQL database in your Coder workspace (or locally), write your queries in VSCode, and submit your work through GitHub Classroom.
By the end of this assignment you will be able to:
statements to retrieve specific columns from a table
to filter rows based on conditions
to sort results in ascending and descending order
Accept the assignment. This creates a private repository for you.
Clone your repository in the terminal:
with the URL GitHub gives you after accepting.
Open the cloned folder in VSCode.
You will write 20 SQL queries against a
database. The database has one table:
Open VSCode and install these two extensions (if you do not already have them):
In the VSCode terminal, create and activate a virtual environment:

python3 -m venv venv
source venv/bin/activate
pip install -r requirements.txt
at the start of your terminal prompt.
Activate the virtual environment every time you open a new terminal window before running any Python commands.
With the virtual environment active, run the autograder once:
The first run will automatically create the
database and load all the data. You will see the message:
Database not found — creating and loading data...
This is expected. After that, every question will show a red X because you have not written any queries yet.
in the left sidebar to open SQLTools.
You can now run queries interactively from within VSCode.
Each file has a comment at the top describing the question and the expected output columns. Write your SQL query on the blank line below the comments.

-- Question 1: List all manga titles in the database, ordered alphabetically.
-- Expected columns: title
You would add your query so the file becomes:
The results will appear in a panel below.
This lets you test and debug your queries before grading.
Run the autograder at any time:
: your query must return exactly the correct columns and rows to earn the 5 points.
Title and author of every manga
Titles and average ratings, highest to lowest
Titles of all manga that are ‘Ongoing’
Title and volumes where volumes > 20, by volumes descending
Manga published 2010 or later, title and year
Manga with an average rating of exactly 5.0
Titles and genres of manga in the ‘Shonen’ genre
Title, author, year of manga published before 2000, by year ascending
Titles starting with the letter ‘N’, ordered alphabetically
Manga NOT in the ‘Shonen’ genre, ordered by genre
Manga published between 2000 and 2010 (inclusive), by year
Manga with rating between 3.0 and 4.0 (inclusive), by rating descending
Manga that are ‘Completed’ OR have avg_rating > 4.8, by title
The autograder also runs automatically on GitHub each time you push. You can view your results in the
When you are done (or want to save progress):

git add -A
git commit -m "Submit Homework 1"
git push
You can push as many times as you like. Only your latest push is graded.
and the data is wrong, reset it:
